messages.body_plain:
This is not an epi question.  You may want to place it into the general 
list.
If you were to do that, I would have pointed you to gc()  :-)

Regards,

Francisco

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] memory over load?
messages.body_plain:
Hi Lynn,

You are posting to the Epi Special Interest Group list in R (R-Sig-Epi). 
  This is a list to post specific question about epidemiological 
analysis using R.  If you have a general question about using R, you 
should subscribe to the general R-Help list.  For a general a 
introduction about the different R-lists, please visit this website 
http://www.r-project.org/mail.html

Type ?gc into the R console-> hit Enter and you will see the answer to 
your second question.

I hope this helps,

Francisco

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] memory over load?
messages.body_plain:
On Thu, 2 Aug 2007, FZ wrote:

> This is not an epi question.  You may want to place it into the general
> list.
> If you were to do that, I would have pointed you to gc()  :-)

And more people would have been available to point out that gc() is not the solution -- gc() is always run before allocating large chunks of memory and before concluding that R is out of memory, so gc() will not help.

         -thomas


> Regards,
>
> Francisco
>
> Lynn Disney wrote:
>> I have been running logistic regression on R and am getting memory
>> overload issues. Is there a way to clear out the memory without
>> restarting R so that I can save my work? I have tried already rm(model1)
>> to remove the work from the first model so that I can run the second,
>> but it doesn't clear out enough memory.

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] memory over load?
messages.body_plain:
Hi Thomas,

My experience in memory management in R is very limited so I am sure you 
are right, but how do you then explain the following behavior starting 
with a fresh session?

memory.size()# 12,000Kb
x=integer(100000000)
memory.size() #approx 412,000kb
rm(x)
memory.size()#still approx 412,000kb
gc()
memory.size()#down to approx 20,000Kb


I am running R version 2.5.1 (2007-06-27), i386-pc-mingw32

Thanks,

Francisco

Hi Thomas,

memory.size()# 12,0

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] memory over load?
messages.body_plain:
On Thu, 2 Aug 2007, FZ wrote:

> Hi Thomas,
>
> My experience in memory management in R is very limited so I am sure you are 
> right, but how do you then explain the following behavior starting with a fresh 
> session?
>
> memory.size()# 12,000Kb
> x=integer(100000000)
> memory.size() #approx 412,000kb
> rm(x)
> memory.size()#still approx 412,000kb
> gc()
> memory.size()#down to approx 20,000Kb
>


Yes, running gc() does affect the results of memory.size(), and running it more than once may have an effect, because there are three levels of garbage collection.

However, a highest-level garbage collection is always done before R gives up on allocating memory.  If you run out of memory in R, adding gc() calls to your code will not help. The original question was about running out of memory.

     -thomas




Thomas Lumley			Assoc. Professor, Biostatistics
tlumley at u.washington.edu	University of Washington, Seattle

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] memory over load?
messages.body_plain:
Thanks for your reply Thomas.  I do understand that is the case once R 
runs out of memory. I did not know about the three levels of garbage 
collection though, thanks for pointing that out (I read the description 
in the R-Internals).
However, my impression was that Lynn wanted to know how to free memory 
to *avoid* running out of memory when running a second model ("I have 
tried already rm(model1) to remove the work from the first model so that 
I can run the second, but it doesn't clear out enough memory.")

Lynn, perhaps you can be more specific about your question?

Regards,

Francisco

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] lognormal distribution in survreg
messages.body_plain:
I fit a lognormal model to survival data using survreg.  I am confused about the output, because I do not know how to interprete the
"scale." The output is:

Call:
survreg(formula = surv1 ~ p$score, dist = "lognormal")
               Value Std. Error     z         p
(Intercept)   5.5891     0.1986 28.14 2.76e-174
p$score      -0.0162     0.0102 -1.58  1.14e-01
Log(scale)    0.4046     0.0594  6.81  9.65e-12

Scale= 1.5

Log Normal distribution
Loglik(model)= -996.1   Loglik(intercept only)= -997.4
        Chisq= 2.5 on 1 degrees of freedom, p= 0.11
Number of Newton-Raphson Iterations: 4
n= 505

Is scale the scale parameter of the lognormal distribution ? If yes is there no estimate of the standard derivation? 

Thanks,

Mariana
messages.body_snippet:

"scale." The output is:

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] lognormal distribution in survreg (Mariana Wagner)
messages.body_plain:
Hi Mariana,

The log(scale)=0.40546 output means that if you want to get the scale,
then, you compute the exponential of the coefficients 0.40546 and the
answer will be 1.5
The sclae for the standard error can be found by  computing the
exponential of 0.0594, giving 1.0611996

Is that clear?

Kind regards,


Lazarus Mramba

P.O Box 230, 80108,
Kilifi, Kenya
Mobile No. +254721292370

(office extension : 419)

Message: 1
Date: Sat, 04 Aug 2007 09:51:33 +0200
From: Mariana Wagner <marianaw at web.de>
Subject: [R-sig-Epi] lognormal distribution in survreg
To: r-sig-epi at stat.math.ethz.ch 
Message-ID: <1592232557 at web.de>
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=iso-8859-15

I fit a lognormal model to survival data using survreg.  I am confused
about the output, because I do not know how to interprete the
"scale." The output is:

Call:
survreg(formula = surv1 ~ p$score, dist = "lognormal")
               Value Std. Error     z         p
(Intercept)   5.5891     0.1986 28.14 2.76e-174
p$score      -0.0162     0.0102 -1.58  1.14e-01
Log(scale)    0.4046     0.0594  6.81  9.65e-12

Scale= 1.5

Log Normal distribution
Loglik(model)= -996.1   Loglik(intercept only)= -997.4
        Chisq= 2.5 on 1 degrees of freedom, p= 0.11
Number of Newton-Raphson Iterations: 4
n= 505

Is scale the scale parameter of the lognormal distribution ? If yes is
there no estimate of the standard derivation? 

Thanks,

Mariana



------------------------------

_______________________________________________
R-sig-Epi mailing list
R-sig-Epi at stat.math.ethz.ch 
https://stat.ethz.ch/mailman/listinfo/r-sig-epi 


End of R-sig-Epi Digest, Vol 16, Issue 2

Hi Mariana,

The log(scale)=0.40546 output means that if you want to get the scale,
then, you compute the exponential of the coefficients 0.40546 and the
answer will be 1.5
The sclae for the standard

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] lognormal distribution in survreg (Mariana Wagner)
messages.body_plain:
On Mon, 6 Aug 2007, Lazarus Mramba wrote:

> Hi Mariana,
>
> The log(scale)=0.40546 output means that if you want to get the scale,
> then, you compute the exponential of the coefficients 0.40546 and the
> answer will be 1.5

And this 1.5 is the standard deviation of a normal distribution, ie, of 
the log of survival time.

 	-thomas

> The sclae for the standard error can be found by  computing the
> exponential of 0.0594, giving 1.0611996
>
> Is that clear?
>
> Kind regards,
>
>

messages.subject: [R-sig-Epi] Could it be possible to set up an international groupto develop a specific Epi package?
messages.subject_clean: Could it be possible to set up an international groupto develop a specific Epi package?
messages.body_plain:
Dear Zhang Zhi Jie,

The development of an Epi package is actually working quite well, see
www.biostat.ku.dk/~bxc/Epi, and for the epitools package see
http://www.epitools.net/

The former which I am the maintainer of is continuously being developed,
and a new versiondue out in a not too distant future.

Suggestions for facilities are most welcome.

Best,
Bendix
______________________________________________

Bendix Carstensen
Senior Statistician
Steno Diabetes Center
Niels Steensens Vej 2-4
DK-2820 Gentofte
Denmark
+45 44 43 87 38 (direct)
+45 30 75 87 38 (mobile)
+45 44 43 73 13 (fax)
bxc at steno.dk   http://www.biostat.ku.dk/~bxc


> -----Original Message-----
> From: r-sig-epi-bounces at stat.math.ethz.ch 
> [mailto:r-sig-epi-bounces at stat.math.ethz.ch] On Behalf Of zhijie zhang
> Sent: Tuesday, August 21, 2007 1:11 PM
> To: r-sig-epi at stat.math.ethz.ch
> Subject: [R-sig-Epi] Could it be possible to set up an 
> international groupto develop a specific Epi package?
> 
> Dear friends,
>   It seems that the development of epidemiological package is 
> not that good.  Could it be possible to set up an 
> international group to develop a specific Epi package? 
> Spatial Analysis Laboratory (SAL) in the Department of 
> Geography at the University of Illinois (www.sal.uiuc.edu) 
> had done well on spatial data analysis, may we do the similar 
> thing on the field of epidemiology?
>   We may need a leader.
> 
> --
> With Kind Regards,
> 
> oooO:::::::::
> (..):::::::::

Dear Zhang Zhi Jie,

www.biostat.ku.dk/~bxc/Epi, and for the epitools package see
http://www.epitools.net/
